title: Noise adaptive two-way secure deterministic quantum key distribution
authors: Abinash Kar, Ayan Patra, Aditi Sen De, Tamoghna Das
abstract: We introduce noise-adaptive quantum key distribution (QKD) protocols, in which the honest parties optimize the encoding (state preparation) and decoding (measurement basis) operations according to the noise models affecting the honest subsystems induced by an eavesdropper. This extends conventional QKD schemes that employ fixed encoding and decoding strategies independent of the noise characteristics of the communication channel. We investigate three representative protocols: entanglement-based secure dense coding (SDC), the entanglement-free Lucamarini and Mancini (LM05), and a two-way prepare-and-measure Bennett Brassard (BB84) protocols. Using entropic uncertainty relations, we derive the corresponding secret key rates for both adaptive and conventional non-adaptive scenarios under collective attacks. For independent but identical noise acting on the forward and backward transmission channels, as well as for correlated and non-Markovian environments, we identify classes of channels for which adaptive schemes yield enhanced secret key rates for the considered protocols. In contrast, we also determine Pauli channels, including depolarizing and bit flip channels, for which adaptive strategies provide no benefit. We further show that these optimal sets are generally non-unique and can differ substantially from the unitaries that maximize dense-coding capacity in the absence of security constraints. Our results establish noise-adaptive encoding and decoding as a powerful framework for improving secure communication over realistic noisy quantum channels.
summary: The paper introduces a framework for noise-adaptive two-way quantum key distribution where encoding and decoding are dynamically optimized to match channel noise. It demonstrates that this approach can increase secret key rates and noise tolerance for specific channel types like bit-phase flip and amplitude damping.
main_problem: Existing two-way quantum key distribution (QKD) protocols use fixed encoding and decoding strategies that do not account for specific channel noise characteristics. The authors aim to develop and analyze noise-adaptive QKD (NAQKD) protocols that optimize these operations based on the noise model.
main_result: The study identifies specific noise channels, such as bit-phase flip and amplitude damping, where adaptive encoding and decoding significantly enhance secret key rates and noise thresholds. Conversely, it proves that for certain unital channels like depolarizing and bit-flip channels, adaptive strategies provide no advantage.
method: The authors use entropic uncertainty relations and the Devetak-Winter bound to derive closed-form expressions for secret key rates. They optimize the parameters of SU(2) unitary operators for encoding and measurement bases to maximize these rates under collective attacks.
model_or_system: A two-way quantum communication framework involving two honest parties (Alice and Bob) and an eavesdropper (Eve). The system involves a qubit traveling through forward and backward quantum channels subject to various noise models, including Pauli, amplitude damping, and non-Markovian environments.
key_observables: Secret key rate, critical noise threshold, and measurement probabilities.
important_parameters: Encoding unitaries (SU(2) parameters theta, chi, phi), noise strength (p), and the overlap parameter (gamma).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the eavesdropper performs collective attacks and that the honest parties can accurately estimate the noise model of the channel.
figures_summary: Plots of secret key rate versus noise strength for different noise models (Bit Flip, Phase Flip, Bit-Phase Flip, and Amplitude Damping), demonstrating where adaptive strategies outperform non-adaptive ones.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the NAQKD framework, defines the mathematical optimization of encoding/decoding unitaries, provides security proofs using entropic uncertainty relations, compares performance across various noise models (Pauli, amplitude damping), and includes detailed mathematical appendices for protocol verification.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides a framework for optimizing quantum communication protocols by leveraging the specific dynamics of the environment-induced noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (Color online) Key rate (r) (ordinate) obtained with the adaptive and non-adaptive schemes against the noise strength (p) (abscissa) across four noise models (a) bit flip, (b) phase flip, (c) bit-phase flip, and, (d) amplitude damping channels, for all the three quantum key distribution protocols- secure dense coding (SDC), LM05 and two-way BB84. The independent noise acts with equal strength in both the forward and backward transmission channels. (a) For the bit flip channel, the optimal adaptive key rate and the non-adaptive key rate coincide for all three protocols over the whole range of p, indicating no benefit from adaptive encoding (state preparation) and decoding...

FIG. 2: (Color online)Map plot of difference in the key rate between the noise-adaptive and the conventional protocol (∆r in Eq. (40)) for the SDC protocol under a general Pauli noise channel as a function of noise parameters pY (abscissa) and pZ (ordinate), keeping fixed values of pX. Here, the parameters pX, pY, and pZ respectively represent the contributions from bit, bit-phase, and phase flip noises. (a) pX = 0, (b) pX = 0.02, and (c) pX = 0.05. Note that with increasing contributions from bit flip noise elements, i.e.e, pX, we obtain less advantage from the adaptive protocol in the case of a general Pauli channel.

FIG. 3: (Color online) Difference in key rate of adaptive and non-adaptive protocols, ∆r (ordinate), against the noise strength p and the degree of correlations µ (abscissa). The upper panel shows ∆r versus p for fixed values of µ, while the lower panel shows ∆r versus µ for fixed values of p. Figures (a) and (b) correspond to the SDC protocol under partially correlated phase flip noise, figures (c) and (d) to the SDC protocol under partially correlated bit-phase flip noise, and figures (e) and ( f ) to the LM05 protocol under partially correlated bit-phase flip noise.

FIG. 4: (Color online) ∆r (ordinate) with respect to p (abscissa) for fixed values of the non-Markovianity parameter α. (a) and (b) the SDC protocol under NM phase flip noise (a), and NM bit-phase flip noise (b). Under NM bit-phase flip noise, in (c), δr is obtained for the LM05 protocol, while in (d), δr is computed for the two-way BB84 protocol. In each of the plots, the (blue) solid lines correspond to α = 0, long-dashed, dot-dashed, and the short-dashed lines represent the increase of Non Markovianity from α = 0.35, α = 0.7, to α = 1.

FIG. 5: (Color online) Comparison of the lower bounds on the key rates for the noise-adaptive and conventional non-adaptive protocols, as well as the noisy dense coding capacity. The quantities radaptive (orange dotted line), rconventional (blue solid line), and Cnoisy (green dashed line) are plotted as functions of the noise parameter p for (a) bit flip, (b) phase flip, (c) bit-phase flip, and (d) depolarizing channels. In all cases, Cnoisy significantly exceeds both the adaptive and non-adaptive key rates, while the black dotted line denotes the classical limit of dense coding capacity. These plots provide a qualitative comparison of the extent to which classical information transmission...
